text: The camera focuses on the liver and gallbladder, providing a clear view of these anatomical structures.
The liver is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon observes the terminal ileum positioned towards the pelvis, identifies the duodenum and pancreas, and locates the middle colic vessels near the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) with no visible evidence of carcinomatosis.
The surgeon identifies the ileocolic vessel, highlighting its location and orientation within the surgical field.
The right colic artery is observed branching off from the iliacolic artery in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the right colon and appendix, with the ureter visible on the right side in a thin patient.
The ureter is visible, displaying vermiculation, which is the normal peristaltic movement of the ureter.
The surgeon lifts the greater omentum over the liver to expose the right colon mesentery. The ileocolic vessel is placed under tension by pulling it upwards.

The surgeon scores inferior to the ileocolic vessel, creating a window through which an instrument is inserted. The instrument is used to pull up and sweep down, facilitating a medial to lateral dissection.
The surgeon gently sweeps the retroperitoneum away from the surrounding structures, taking care due to the thinness of the tissue.
The surgeon manipulates the paper-thin mesentery, sweeping it downwards while maintaining the orientation of the purple tissue layer above.
The surgeon performs a sweeping motion to move the duodenal sweep downwards, ensuring clear separation from surrounding structures.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the thin tissue, using a technique similar to a breaststroke, pulling up with one hand while gently sweeping down with the other to avoid poking through the delicate mesentery.
The surgeon maneuvers underneath the colon, carefully navigating the thin tissue layers.
The surgeon identifies the plane and prepares to divide the ileocolic artery, while gently sweeping the duodenum away from the surgical field using a sweeping motion.
The surgeon uses an energy device to divide the ileocolic vessel, completing the separation of this vascular structure from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon holds the camera steady while lifting the colon to reveal the transverse colon mesentery. The superior mesenteric artery (SMA) is visible, and the transverse colon is identified upon looking up.
The surgeon places the colon under tension, revealing multiple branches of the mesenteric vessels. The dissection progresses from the left side to access the correct anatomical plane.
The surgeon navigates through the hepatic flexure, revealing the underlying duodenum.
The surgeon identifies one of the middle colic pedicles, specifically noting the right branch, within the surgical field.
The surgeon repositions the small bowel, moving it out of the pelvis to improve visibility. The sigmoid colon is identified and exposed.
The surgeon lifts the tissue from the bottom, creating tension to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon pulls the greater omentum upwards, separating it from the transverse colon.
The surgeon identifies the colon mesentery and pulls it downward, while searching for the stomach to reposition it.
The surgeon begins to take down the omentum, moving closer to the colon, as part of the dissection process.
The surgeon manipulates the anterior leaf of the greater omentum, revealing the gastroepiploic vessels and the colon mesentery, which is visibly connected to the colon.
The surgeon identifies the gastroepiploic vessel and divides it, ensuring to stay clear of the surrounding structures before proceeding to the upper area of the surgical field.
The surgical team adjusts the patient into the Trendelenburg position, tilting the operating table to enhance access to the surgical site.
The surgeon identifies the ileum, noting that it is fused together with surrounding structures. The camera angle is adjusted to the right to improve the view of the surgical field.

The surgeon begins dissection at the sacral promontory, using the left hand to grasp the tissue where the incision will be made. Tension is applied to facilitate cutting, and adjustments are made to create a larger window by moving further to the left.
The surgeon identifies the left iliac vessel and maneuvers above it, pushing it upward to access the plane behind it. The surgeon prepares to cut through the tissue plane to divide the structure.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the ureter, holding it up slightly to ensure clear visibility and avoid nerve damage during the dissection.
The surgeon identifies and manipulates the branches of the hypogastric nerve, while adjusting the ureter to a lower position for better access.
The surgeon identifies the left kidney and prepares to manipulate or dissect the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon gently sweeps down the thin tail of the pancreas, which is being pulled up, ensuring it is repositioned appropriately.
The surgical field shows the disconnected structures, with one remaining element still attached.
The surgeon repositions the colon back into its anatomic position, adjusting the left side to ensure proper alignment within the surgical field.
The surgeon pulls a structure to the right, adjusting its position within the surgical field.
The surgeon requests a straight bowel clamp while considering the use of a GIA stapler, indicating the presence of two structures requiring attention.
The surgeon requests a straight bowel clamp and proceeds to cut the specimen, indicating the removal of a section of the bowel.
